text: The robotic surgical system is used to perform a multivisceral resection on a patient with a T4N1 tumor. The small bowel, visibly involved with the tumor, is stapled off and left attached to the specimen. The DaVinci X system and Shoreform X-T stapler with a blue cartridge are employed to divide the small bowel. The robotic vessel sealer is used to divide the mesentery, and the efferent loop of the small bowel is divided with a second stapler firing.

The surgeon begins dissection at the sacral promontory, identifying the iliac vessels, ureter, and inferior mesenteric artery (IMA). Dissection proceeds cranially from below the IMA towards its origin at the aorta. Using Endo-wrist instruments, the sympathetic nerves are visualized, and safe dissection is performed around the IMA. The artery is skeletonized, clipped with Hemlock clips applied by the assistant from the right-side port, and then divided.

The surgeon focuses on the inferior mesenteric vein near the DJ flexure, defining the anatomy in this area. A medial to lateral dissection is performed to separate the colonic mesentery from Girota's fascia, with the assistant providing counter-direction on the paranephric fat. The inferior mesenteric vein is clipped with hemlock clips and divided under the pancreas. Dissection proceeds cranially to separate the colon and enter the lesser sac in front of the pancreas. Further dissection separates the inferior mesenteric artery from the gonadal vessels and left ureter, with visible inflammation and edema due to cancer and tumor desmoplasia. The lateral release of the left colon and sigmoid is facilitated by traction and counter-traction, making the dissection straightforward.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealing instrument to finalize the adhesion between the spleen and the colon, employing bipolar energy to securely divide vessels up to seven millimeters in size.

The surgeon focuses on the pelvis, performing posterior dissection in the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane. The right inferior hypogastric nerve is visible at the five o'clock position. The bladder, involved with cancer, is entered full thickness, revealing a malignant fistula to the colon and a Foley catheter in the bladder lumen. On the right side, the round ligament and ovarian pedicle are divided using a gas sealing device. The uterus, with large fibroids, is prepared for en bloc hysterectomy. Similar dissection is performed on the left side to mobilize the left colon and divide the left tubo-ovarian pedicle.

The surgeon completes bladder dissection using a vessel sealing device. The uterus is pulled upwards to develop a plane behind the bladder and uterus, causing minor bleeding managed by suction. The specimen is mobilized cranially, creating space in the pelvis for mesorectal transection and rectum division with a robotic stapler. A single firing of a 60 Shoreform stapler transects the rectum, releasing the specimen by dividing remaining adhesions. The final view of the pelvis shows both ureters, with the vaginal vault closed using a running 3-0 V-lock suture. Partial cystectomy results in a transection line near the trigone, with ureters stented to prevent capture during bladder suturing. The bladder is closed in two layers with a 3-0 V-lock suture, facilitated by Endo-wrist instruments.
